cards.back: A variable defined inside a function (including function parameters). Local variables have block scope and automatic duration.

cards.text: An identifier's {{c1::scope}} determines where the identifier can be accessed within the source code.

cards.back: A variable with block scope can only be accessed from the point of declaration until the end of the block in which it is declared (including nested blocks).

cards.front: Can a variable defined in an outer block be accessed from a nested block?
cards.back: Yes. Variables defined in an outer block can be seen inside nested blocks. However, variables in nested blocks cannot be seen in outer blocks.

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Variable x goes out of scope at the end of the nested block, so it cannot be accessed afterwards.
cards.explanation: Variables with block scope are destroyed at the end of the block in which they are defined. Trying to use x outside its block causes a compilation error.

cards.back: A variable's duration determines when it is created and destroyed. Duration is a runtime property.
Variables with automatic duration are created at the point of definition and destroyed at the end of the block in which they are defined.

cards.text: Local variables have {{c1::block scope}} and {{c2::automatic duration}} by default.

cards.front: Where are function parameters created and destroyed?
cards.back: Function parameters are created when the function is entered and destroyed when the function is exited, even though they are not defined inside the function block.

cards.front: What are two key reasons to define variables in the smallest/most limited scope possible?
cards.back:
• Reduces the complexity of the program by limiting where variables can be used
  • Reduces the chance of naming collisions since fewer identifiers are in scope at any point

cards.concept: When to define variables close to first use
cards.explanation: Define variables in the most limited existing scope. If that's not possible, create a new block to limit the scope. Define variables as close to their first use as reasonable to make code easier to understand and reduce the window for errors.

cards.text: Variables with {{c1::automatic}} duration are created at the point of {{c2::definition}} and destroyed when the {{c3::block}} ends.
Variables in an {{c1::outer block}} can be seen in {{c2::nested blocks}}, but variables in nested blocks cannot be seen in the outer block.
Define variables in the {{c1::most limited scope}} possible and as {{c2::close to their first use}} as reasonable.
Function parameters are created when the function is {{c1::entered}} and destroyed when the function {{c2::exits}}.
